steps.title: Square both sides to eliminate the radical
steps.content:
Starting with $\sqrt{k - x} = 28 - x$, square both sides to convert to a quadratic equation. The squaring operation preserves any solution where both sides have the same sign, but may introduce extraneous solutions.

$$k - x = (28 - x)^2$$
$$k - x = 784 - 56x + x^2$$
$$x^2 - 55x + (784 - k) = 0$$

The solutions to this quadratic are valid for the original equation only if they satisfy $x \leq k$ (so the square root is defined) and $x \leq 28$ (so the right side is non-negative, matching the non-negative left side).

steps.title: Analyze the quadratic using the discriminant
steps.content:
For the quadratic $x^2 - 55x + (784 - k) = 0$, the discriminant is:

$$\Delta = 55^2 - 4(784 - k) = 3025 - 3136 + 4k = 4k - 111$$

The number of real roots depends on $\Delta$:
  • If $4k - 111 < 0$: no real solutions to the quadratic
  • If $4k - 111 = 0$: exactly one real solution to the quadratic
  • If $4k - 111 > 0$: two distinct real solutions to the quadratic
For the original equation to have exactly one real solution, either the quadratic has one double root that satisfies the constraints, OR it has two roots but only one satisfies $x \leq \min(k, 28)$.

steps.title: Find when a double root satisfies the constraints
steps.content:
When the discriminant equals zero: $4k - 111 = 0$, giving $k = \frac{111}{4} = 27.75$.

The double root is:
$$x = \frac{55}{2} = 27.5$$

Check the constraints for $k = 27.75$ and $x = 27.5$:
  • Is $x \leq k$? Yes: $27.5 \leq 27.75$ ✓
  • Is $x \leq 28$? Yes: $27.5 \leq 28$ ✓
Verify in the original equation: $\sqrt{27.75 - 27.5} = 28 - 27.5 \Rightarrow \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5 \Rightarrow 0.5 = 0.5$ ✓

So $k = 27.75$ yields exactly one real solution. For this value, $4k = 111$.

steps.title: Verify that k = 27.75 gives the minimum value of 4k
steps.content:
For $k < 27.75$: The discriminant is negative, so there are no real roots — the original equation has zero solutions. ✗

For $27.75 < k < 28$: The discriminant is positive. Both roots satisfy $x \leq k$ and $x \leq 28$, so the original equation has two solutions. ✗

For $k = 28$: We get $x_1 = 27$ and $x_2 = 28$. Both satisfy the constraints, so there are two solutions. ✗

For $k > 28$: The larger root $x_2 = \frac{55 + \sqrt{4k - 111}}{2} > 28$ (since it equals 28 when $k = 28$ and increases thereafter), so it fails the constraint $x \leq 28$. Only the smaller root $x_1 < 27.5 < 28$ is valid. This gives exactly one solution, but $4k > 111$. ✓

The smallest value of $k$ for which the equation has exactly one real solution is $k = 27.75$, so the minimum value of $4k$ is $\boxed{111}$.
